methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $350M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Quantum Computing

private.name: Quantum Machines
private.rationale: Same sector (Quantum Computing), same stage (Series C)

data.narrative:
Oxford Quantum Circuits is a Series C quantum computing company that has raised $350M to date. The broader quantum computing sector contains 11 comparables across public and private markets: 3 public names (IonQ, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum) and 8 private companies including IonQ, Quantinuum, and Infleqtion.

The peer-median method proved inapplicable due to insufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables—only 1 adjacent-stage peer was identified within the quantum computing sector, falling short of the 3-datapoint threshold for reliable triangulation. Consequently, the analysis relies on stage-progression methodology, which applies a 1.7x step-up multiplier typical for Series C advancement to the $350M total raised proxy, yielding an implied valuation of $595M.

This single-method triangulation produces a median valuation estimate of $595M, with a low-end scenario of $506M (assuming a more conservative multiplier application) and a high-end scenario of $684M (applying upside variance). The analysis carries material uncertainty given the nascent and heterogeneous nature of the quantum computing market and the absence of reliable peer-derived multiples. Validation would benefit from direct comparables at identical funding stages and comparable technical or commercial milestones, which currently do not exist in sufficient quantity.
